data.title: GGN: Attractive For Income But Faces Energy Sector Risks
data.description:
GAMCO Global Gold, Natural Resources & Income Trust (NYSE:GGN), a closed-end fund, managed by Gabelli, invests in energy and precious metal stocks, adding covered calls. The market price tends to follow the posted asset value most recently at $5.0. Over long periods of time, this investment hasn't been stellar, but during periods of more inflationary environments, this is almost a must. We, for a significant period of time, owned a sizable lot, receiving the nice $0.03 per month dividend. Our last article, GGN: Recommend Patience And Strategic Trading, outlined a trading strategy selling in the middle $4s. We did sell and invest in volatile tech companies with their rich covered calls. Of note, we do own a rather large percentage of assets in gold and silver bullion or coinage. We aren't with exposure. Up front, we would not buy this fund, but if you still own, in our view, it is not clearly a sell unless you have other opportunities. We rate this a hold for now, like getting paid while holding that precious grandchild.

Markets for Gold/Precious Metals and Energy

Beginning with gold, which is now driving the fund price, a chart follows.

Regardless of what others might read in the chart, it truly is a breakout without indicating upward bounds. Investors won't know from the chart, the top.

Next, a look at gold prices vs. Fed rates is included.

At times, a very reverse or negative relationship between the two influences the price. The Fed seems ready to begin unwinding high-interest rates closer to 3% over the next several months. Others write,

However, there’s little evidence that gold is consistently weakened by federal funds rate hikes or Treasury yields. Despite some negative correlation in the 2000s, there are far too many instances of gold and interest rates rising together to say that high interest rates cause gold prices to fall.

In the real world, more than one factor drives prices, such as demand from economic fears, but Fed moves can't be ignored.

Goldman Sachs wrote this note to investors,

And while many big-name pundits see gold rising to $3,800 in the medium term, which entails another 3% in upside from current levels, Goldman Sachs (GS) actually thinks the metal could be headed to $5,000 per ounce next year if the Fed’s independence is compromised. For now, $5,000 per ounce is highly conditional and is more of a bull-case scenario than anything else.

We are certain that no one can define the concept "compromised Fed." The original legislation grants a very real and significant power for the President of the United States to disrupt it as seen fit. We expect major changes over the next several months with respect to who is on the board, major. If Goldman is correct, investors may expect much higher gold and silver prices. We believe that they are.

Oil/Energy

This fund also invests in energy companies such as Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX). An article in OilPrice noted that the IEA, a wayward organization, "is about to revise its peak oil demand scenario radically..." In the past, this organization carried water for the global elite, panning climate change, CO₂, and energy, claiming that the sky is falling. IEA was the rogue actor. Apparently, a change of allegiance prompted this radical departure. Crude oil and other fossil fuels aren't peaking in demand anytime soon. OPEC+ and IEA publicly clashed often over the past several years. Reality set in, forcing the change of mind. With more common certainty of crude demand, the issue becomes with whom. Reuters noted that "growth was coming from non-OPEC countries in North and South America, notably Canada, Guyana, Brazil, and Argentina, as well as Suriname." Interestingly noted, the U.S. is missing from future growth; focusing the fund investments must have a measured exposure to other Western Hemisphere countries. And Reuters also noted that growth will have political consequences.

Volume growth, at some level, isn't the issue. The issue remains prices driven by supply/demand balances. On the issue, the news seems split, but the consensus for now points to glut. Again, from OilPrice, we found this,

Goldman Sachs expects the global oil market to swing into a surplus of 1.9 million barrels daily next year as a result of OPEC+’s output cut unwinding and producers in North and South America also ramping up production.

There is a very strong argument for mid-$50s priced crude coming perhaps next year. This won't bode well for production-based energy investments.

The Fund

This closed-end investment includes both of the described investments, currently heavily weighted toward precious metals. The latest semi-annual reports reveal that precious metals are 55%, energy is 32%, the balance is in bonds and cash. The reports also include a chart outlining the investment returns.

This summary shows the cyclical nature of the investment.

Reviewing Risks and Rewards with Short Option Investments

The strategy of writing covered calls provides cash for paying dividends. It also adds risk, especially with stocks that run significantly past strike prices. With gold climbing through the roof, a review of a couple of gold-driven stocks seems in order. We choose Newmont (NEM) and Barrick (B). For Newmont, the latest reports show approximately 600,000 shares with calls at the mid-$60s expiring between October and December. The stock price ran through $65 several weeks ago and is now trading at the mid-$80s.

A review of Barrick illustrates a similar path. The fund holds just less than 600,000 shares, with most of the shares covered with short calls ranging between $21-$25 expiring between July and November. The current price is $29, significantly higher than the strike prices.

Reviewing a couple of energy stocks now seems in order, beginning with Exxon. At the end of June, the fund held approximately 450,000 shares. Short calls written against ranged in price from $110 to $120 with expirations between July and January of 2026. Interestingly, the lower strike prices expire in the later part of 2025 and early 2026. There is a natural cyclical period in crude pricing; thus, the models expect lower stock pricing in winter months. The current price is approximately $115.

Continuing, the fund holds approximately 175,000 shares of Chevron. The strike prices were between $150 and $160, with the lower-priced strikes expiring in the January 26 timeframe. The closing price on the 19th of September was approximately $155, again in the ranges of the strike prices.

The precious metal investments have seemingly run above the strikes reported at the end of June. The energy sector provides cash from written short calls. The fund pays out $0.03 per month, or approximately $5 million in dividends. There are also expenses.

What To Watch

In closed funds, asset growth comes through internal growth. When short call strategies are used and prices run ahead of the strikes, long-term asset damage can occur. Managers have choices. During one period earlier in the year, the fund lost a significant portion of Newmont but found a soft price period for repurchasing. This is one lever. A second lever uses longer option dates to roll the current options higher without incurring loss of stock. This eats into cash generation. The cash/bond positions pay approximately $5 million per year in interest, or one month's worth. The balance must come through short calls or dividends.

The next quarterly report will show investors a lot about what approaches management chose to cover the cash outflows. Again, the risk equals losing long-term capital through ending up with fewer shares of individual stocks. When gold heads south, this matters.

The Chart

Next, we provide a day chart of the fund value from TradeStation Securities. Remember, for the most part, the price follows closely the NAV.

Notice that the chart increase is following, at some ratio, the price of gold.

Risk & Investment

In our view, the risk resides with energy going forward, at least through the balance of the year. Crude oil, thus the energy holdings, tends to follow cyclical patterns, being weak in the winter months. We have read estimates of $50 per barrel pricing or something close this year. The fund's energy holdings will suffer. We also expect precious metals to continue higher with the risk that stock prices might continue to run ahead of strikes. In this environment, holding the asset size, requires strategies that either cost cash generated elsewhere, or shunt cash generation. The September report will shed a lot of light on this issue.

But the asset value has been growing with metal prices and its respective value. The above TradeStation chart strongly suggests this outcome. With prices likely canceling each other out, lower energy, higher precious metals, we can't recommend a buy. We also, can't recommend selling with the nice dividend. As noted above, we no longer own the fund but continue to follow it. It is performing according to design, strong upward momentum during periods of bullish metal and energy pricing with a nice dividend. We have no plans to rebuy unless the price falls back to under $4 still within continued bullish sentiment. We doubt that that will happen anytime soon. Again, we have a hold on the fund.

Oh, and holding that grandchild is still a joy, at least for now, until it isn't.

data.title: Martin Marietta Materials: On Aggregate Looking Too Expensive
data.description:
At the start of 2024, I concluded that Martin Marietta Materials (NYSE:MLM) was adding more aggregates, yet concluded that issues were lingering. In the one and a half years that followed, shares have seen about 15% returns, just half those seen by the market, marking quite some underperformance.

At the time, shares hit fresh highs, yet I was cautious with the market awarding ever higher multiples to the solid operator, creating a tough balancing act for investors to perform.

That is after a very strong track record, yet it also comes after a few years in which margins have expanded a great deal further, raising the question of what the realistic outlook for margins could be.

Aggregate Materials

Martin Marietta Materials runs a diversified aggregates business, complemented by certain specialty products. The company employs nearly 10,000 workers, who generate $7.0 billion in sales from the production and sale of aggregates from some 400 quarries, mines, and yards, these totaling some 200 million tons per year.

These revenues are generated from some three segments, with aggregates at large making up about two-thirds of sales. This is complemented by a near 30% cement and downstream business, while 5% of sales are generated from specialty products. On aggregate, these are very profitable operations, with EBITDA margins seen around a third of sales.

These aggregates can be found all over the country and spur 85 years of reserves based on current production levels, with downstream and specialty products including cement, asphalt, lime, magnesia products, found in more targeted places across the country.

Going Back To Early 2024

When I last looked at the shares early in 2024, Martin Marietta was on track to post record revenues of around $6.8 billion for 2023. Shares were valued at $537 per share, granting the company a $33 billion equity valuation and $37 billion enterprise valuation. At such levels the business was valued at around 5.5 times sales, 17-18 times EBITDA, and with earnings seen at around $18 per share, earnings multiple came in at around 30 times. Much of this was driven by pricing power, as volumes were actually negative at the time.

The company touted long-term demand drivers from Federal Highway Investments, CHIPS Act, and general government spending, as there were some moving targets in its business as well. Late in 2023, the company sold its South Texas cement business in a $2.1 billion deal to CRH Plc, with the money used to buy 20 aggregate operations from Blue Water Industries in a $2.05 billion deal early in 2024.

While I liked the track record, I was fearful about paying over 30 times earnings for an aggregates business, and the fact that growth was driven by pricing instead of volumes, also taking into account that shares had seen some 80% returns over the past year alone, pushing up expectations.

Lagging

In between the M&A action and market developments, Martin Marietta reported a 4% fall in 2024 sales to $6.5 billion at the start of the year. Shipments were down 4% to 191 million tons, yet average prices jumped 10% to $22 per ton, indicating a continuation of the mix of higher prices and lower volumes.

Adjusted for the divestment gains, adjusted earnings came in around $18 per share, all largely in line with expectations. Net debt was up to $4.7 billion, largely the result of the Blue Water deal, yet with adjusted EBITDA exceeding two billion, that was not an issue.

The company guided for 2025 sales to advance to a midpoint of $7.03 billion, plus or minus two hundred million, with adjusted EBITDA seen around $2.25 billion.

Growth returned in the first quarter of 2025, with sales up 8% to $1.35 billion, yet quarterly earnings only came in at $1.90 per share in what seasonally is always a softer quarter. Second quarter sales were up 3% to $1.81 billion, with growth driven by pricing instead of volumes, as quarterly earnings rose to $5.43 per share, with growth rates reported up in the mid-teens.

Sales are now seen at a midpoint of $6.97 billion, yet the EBITDA midpoint was hiked to $2.30 billion. This is welcomed as net debt ticked up to $5.2 billion due to continued share buybacks and higher capital expenditures.

By now, the 60 million shares trade at $613 per share, pushing up the enterprise valuation to $42 billion. This values the business at around 6 times sales, 18 times EBITDA, and leaves shares trading at an earnings multiple in the low thirties.

Another Bolt-On Deal

Days before the second quarter earnings release, Martin Marietta Materials reported a $450 million cash deal to acquire Quickrete Holdings, an aggregate producer with a production capacity of 20 million tons.

Based on purchase price, this is a bolt-on deal, valued at 1% of the valuation of Martin Marietta, although it comes with an outsized production addition to the overall business, although average selling prices have not been communicated. This would increase pro forma net debt to $5.7 billion, still very manageable with leverage ratios seen in the mid-2s.

Days later, Martin reported a 5% increase in the quarterly dividend, now payable at $0.83 per share each quarter, with the dividend yield only amounting to 0.5% here.

And Now?

My conclusion from early 2024 remains largely the same today. While modest growth has been seen and bolt-on M&A is pursued, volumes are not growing, as shares have moved up largely in tandem or, frankly, exceeded the operating performance a bit. This makes that premium valuations remain very premium today, which makes it so that despite the lagging returns in comparison to the wider market, this makes it very hard to be upbeat here and see a compelling risk-reward yet.

While I understand and appreciate the appeal of such businesses, in which cash flows can become much higher than reported earnings, I am awaiting a better entry point at a lower multiple here before getting involved, as in normal conditions earnings should more closely track cash flows.

However, I am keen to keep a close eye on the developments from here onwards, but simply fail to see appeal at current levels.

data.title: Modern Warehouses Driving The Next Phase Of Industrial Growth
data.description:
By Art Jones, Senior Director, Global Real Estate Research and Strategy

Despite elevated vacancy rates, the industrial sector remains fundamentally healthy, particularly in modern warehouse facilities. The current softness reflects a cyclical mismatch between supply and leasing velocity, rather than structural weakness. With the construction pipeline contracting and demand for modern logistics assets holding firm, vacancy rates are likely to normalize sooner than expected. As new supply bottoms out by 2026–27, conditions appear favorable for the next development cycle to emerge.

Warehouse net absorption and vacancy by facility age cohort

By facility age, 2020–present

The industrial sector has recently faced headwinds from excess development in select markets and weaker demand tied to policy uncertainty, most notably newly implemented tariffs. These pressures have pushed vacancy rates higher, as new projects came online just as corporate tenants delayed long-term leasing commitments. While this clouds the near-term outlook, it more likely represents temporary cyclical dislocation rather than a sign of structural weakness.

Even so, modern warehouses have remained resilient. Recently built facilities continue to attract strong demand, keeping the sector among the most favored by institutional investors. From 2020 to 2024, warehouses constructed since 2010 averaged 177 million square feet of annual net absorption, well above the long-term average of 103 million. Year-to-date in 2025, this cohort has already absorbed 50 million square feet. Although the current vacancy rate of 15.5% appears elevated, much of it reflects properties still in lease-up rather than stabilized assets underperforming.

Looking ahead, the construction pipeline is expected to keep contracting while demand for modern logistics facilities holds steady. Strong lease-up activity, even in today’s environment, suggests that vacancy will improve more quickly than headlines imply, setting the stage for a more durable recovery. By 2026–27, when new supply is set to reach its lowest point since the Global Financial Crisis, conditions will likely warrant the start of a new development cycle, positioning the sector for its next phase of growth.

Original Post

data.title: Electronic Arts: Sell The Takeover News
data.description:
On Friday, Electronic Arts (EA) went up 15% on news that private equity firm Silver Lake, along with the Saudi Arabia investment fund, is looking to take EA private for as much as $50 billion.

With the news, EA stock leapt to a new all-time high:

EA Stock: Not Much Point In Holding

There is limited upside left in the stock based on what we know now. Assuming a transaction does go through for exactly $50 billion, that would work out to about $199 per share in compensation based on the company's 251 million share count as of last quarter. That's only a few percent potential gain in return for a likely holding period of at least a few months before the transaction closed.

In the event that the deal does not close, there could be considerable downside here. The stock was trading at $170 on Friday morning, for one thing, so that's a much bigger potential decline than the upside if the deal goes through at $199. And earlier this year, EA stock traded as low as $115. Shares had already rallied dramatically prior to the takeover news.

Furthermore, I believe private equity and the Saudi investment fund would be overpaying dramatically if they struck a deal at this price. Here's why I think the deal is likely to produce poor returns for buyers and carries considerable risk for shareholders if the M&A talks fizzle out.

The Video Game Industry Has Changed

The video game has seen a seismic shift in its business model over the past 15 years.

It used to be that big publishers would have a wide catalog of individual games coming out every year. The thinking was similar to movies or music albums. You make a lot of different titles every year. A few will flop, a number will recoup costs or produce a modest profit, and a handful will be blockbusters. Those blockbusters would more than carry the whole operation. Underwrite a lot of unique games across different genres and see what catches on with consumers in any given year.

However, the games industry has increasingly been taken over by finance people and spreadsheet operators rather than gamers or artistic types. This has meant a shift in business focus toward greenlighting projects with a high probability of steady cash flows and predictable returns on investment. Under this thinking, it's better to double down on proven intellectual property and reliable monetization models rather than taking a swing at a new innovation or an out-of-favor game genre.

To EA's credit, this business model pivot worked well over the past decade:

Between 2005 and 2015, EA barely grew its topline at all in inflation-adjusted terms, and the stock price largely didn't go anywhere. Over the past decade, however, where EA has emphasized its leading game franchises rather than investing in a wider content library, it has seen strong results. Revenues nearly doubled between 2015 and 2023. EA stock blasted off.

Since 2023, however, EA's results have come back down to earth. Revenue has stagnated. And the firm's hyper focus on live service model games in particular appears to be starting to run thin.

Earlier this year, fellow Seeking Alpha author Kevin Anthony D. Arroyo wrote a piece on EA titled "How Risk Aversion Is Slowing It Down" which highlighted how companies like EA have largely stopped taking risks on new games and IP in order to protect near-term financial results.

I fully agree with that argument. In my view, EA is increasingly relying on just a few core titles to keep delivering revenue year in and year out rather than creating exciting new games and intellectual property. A certain type of gamer will buy the new EA soccer or football game every year, but these games typically get middling reviews and don't move the needle in terms of advancing the games industry. It's no surprise that Gen Z seems increasingly wary of buying games, given how companies like EA and Activision have shunned making bold new properties in terms of focusing on bland but dependable revenue-generators.

EA Is Now A Live Services And Subscription Model Business

In the latest 12 months of results, shown below, EA generated $5.4 billion in live services revenues and just $2.0 billion from full game sales:

Many investors probably think of games as a transaction where you'd go to Best Buy (BBY) or GameStop (GME), plunk down $50, and pick up the latest and greatest new game. And that was the end of the transaction.

But that sort of game sale (and the equivalent through digital stores) is now a modest fraction of revenues for big publishers like EA. Rather, the big bucks are in selling monthly subscriptions, game passes, and potentially addictive in-game monetization methods such as loot boxes.

It's well-trodden territory to critique EA for how it manages its sports franchises. And sports are something of a unique niche within gaming. So, I'll focus my attention elsewhere.

The Sims 4: A Case Study

I'd highlight another EA property, The Sims 4, for a great example of how EA's lack of innovation is currently driving robust profits but could end up leading to failure over the intermediate-term.

The original Sims and Sims 2 were pioneers of the life simulation genre and are widely-studied among gaming historians. The developer, Maxis, was a highly respected creative shop that launched all sorts of quirky games like SimTower and SimGolf that have long since turned purely into nostalgia fodder and hold no commercial relevance today.

However, Maxis also had its consistently successful SimCity series, which was highly-popular up through SimCity 4.

Under EA's leadership, however, the company decided to make the fifth mainline SimCity game an online-only experience; turning the classic city builder into a live service game with enhanced monetization opportunities that could theoretically deliver strong financial upside. The problem is that city builders tend to be single-player games, and the fan base revolted against the online bells and whistles. The fifth SimCity game was a colossal flop, and EA exited the city-building genre after that. The ground was entirely ceded to Paradox Interactive (OTCPK:PRXXF), which would go on to sell more than 12 million copies of its rival Cities Skylines in the city-building genre that EA had left behind.

City builders aren't that large of a genre, so EA giving up on it isn't the end of the world from a financial perspective.

That said, this could be a bad omen for the Sims as well. That's because EA has seemingly abandoned innovation there as well. The Sims 4 originally came out in 2014, meaning it is more than 11 years old at this point. In August of this year, EA suggested there will not be a Sims 5 anytime soon.

Sims 4, for its part, tends to be buggy and can run poorly if you install a significant number of its seemingly unceasing number of downloaded content packs and other in-game purchasable items.

When I say unceasing, I'm not kidding. As per online game shop Steam, there are now 102 pieces of distinct downloadable content for Sims 4. The overall cost? Well, I'll let you see for yourself:

All Sims 4 available DLC, United States Steam store, as of Sept. 26th, 2025.

It's truly remarkable to think that to get the full experience, it would now cost more than $1,500. Who would pay that much?

The thing is, EA has been releasing new DLCs for this game for 11 years now. So, fans pay $40 here, $20 here, $15 there, and it adds up.

But it becomes intimidating for new players. Who is going to start out as a new player of an 11-year-old game where you'd need to spend a thousand bucks or more to get the full experience? This infinite DLC model for an increasingly outdated game has generated a ton of cash, sure, but it is irritating fans and leaving EA vulnerable to competition from new life sims, just as Paradox dethroned EA as the king of city builder simulations.

To that point, South Korean firm Krafton launched its own life sim game, InZOI, earlier this year and sold more than one million copies in a single week. While InZOI has some issues of its own, it is a fresh, great-looking game competing against an 11-year-old peer. As long as EA seems to think it has a monopoly on the genre and thus underinvests in The Sims franchise, it is ripe to be disrupted. In addition to InZOI, another promising life sim competitor, Paralives, is expected to release in December of this year.

Private equity may see a long, steady string of Sims 4 revenues historically and think these will continue in perpetuity. But the game is more than a decade-old and competition is arriving rather quickly now. As long as EA refuses to invest the tens (perhaps hundreds) of millions of dollars necessary to make a proper sequel, it is leaving the door wide open for a new rival to come in and siphon off much of the player base with a fresh, modern spin on the genre. Just as EA lost SimCity to new and improved competition, I believe it will lose the Sims within the next 5-7 years as well.

EA Needs To Invest More To Maintain Its Moat

I play several new games every month and follow the industry closely. I could go on all day about EA's strategic decisions and how they've garnered substantial consumer backlash across many of their gaming IPs.

I'll sum up, though, and say that after a decade of focusing on juicing profitability and cutting costs, I believe EA is near the maximum possible returns that it could obtain with the current business model.

Franchises like Sims 4 are seemingly worn out and tired at this point. EA risks losing players both to newer and more compelling games and also other alternative entertainment options such as streaming video or social media. We already see that with Gen Z, who is reportedly spending much less on gaming than Millennials do. And Gen Z is more prone to playing one or two games religiously rather than sampling and buying a wider array of different titles. With properties like Fortnite and Minecraft soaking up tons of attention, it leaves less room for EA's games, especially among younger users.

Put simply, it's great that EA has its games that are huge with millennials. But it remains to be seen if it will be able to make similar inroads with Gen Z. And if you're private equity making a long-term bet on EA's portfolio, that's a key question that has to be addressed.

Valuation Seems Rich

I'm concerned about this deal's valuation. Analysts see the company generating adjusted earnings per share of $8.28 in fiscal year 2026. At a $50 billion valuation, this would put EA stock around 24 times adjusted earnings. Not terrible, right?

However, I don't think those adjusted earnings are the best way to look at the company. There's a huge amount of stock compensation here that is getting backed out of those adjusted earnings.

Excluding a one-time tax benefit in 2020, which distorted the numbers, EA's actual GAAP earnings have been pretty consistently between $3 and $4 per share for the past decade:

At a takeout price of $199, representative of a $50 billion valuation, a buyer would be paying close to 50x GAAP earnings. What's going on here? In the most recent quarter, EA reported $450 million in non-GAAP operating income. But it spent $152 million -- more than a third of that -- on stock-based compensation. Throw in some acquisition costs, and actual EPS came in far short of the adjusted figure.

I would guess that a sizable chunk of this stock-based comp will turn into cash salary expenses going forward. If EA doesn't want its skilled developers and senior employees to leave, the stock comp will need to be made up for in some other way to retain talent.

We can debate what portion of the adjustments to earnings makes sense here or not. Regardless, however, I see the 24x adjusted earnings figure as being optimistic and would encourage investors to closely consider EA's historical GAAP earnings when thinking about what this business might be worth in an M&A deal.

EA Stock Bottom Line

The gaming industry is in a challenging position right now. There was a huge downturn in 2023 and 2024. We saw the largest layoffs in the industry's history. And some industry experts I spoke with compared the recent downturn to the Atari crash of 1983, giving a sense of just how unprecedented the recent issues have been.

It remains to be seen how the big games companies will react. Firms like Ubisoft Entertainment (OTCPK:UBSFY) have had a brutal go of it trying to adapt to the times:

Admittedly, Ubisoft has made some uniquely questionable strategic decisions in recent years. Still, I am skeptical that EA's business is worthy of an all-time high valuation, as other gaming companies are on the rocks. And EA, for its part, has seen its growth slow to a crawl as well.

I think EA has already picked most of the low-hanging fruit from the live service model. Counterpart Sony (SONY) has cancelled eight of its 12 live service games that were in development, as the live services market is saturated. I think there are limits to EA's current game plan if it doesn't invest in more top-notch single-player games.

The big publishers, including EA, are at risk of ceding market share to AA and smaller independent studios that still take innovative risks and build games on more modest budgets. There has been a ton of talent fired from the top studios since 2023, meaning that smaller studios can grab world-class developers. We're also seeing increasingly high-quality games debut from studios in countries like China, South Korea, and Poland, which have lower development costs than the United States.

In addition, the rise of top-notch game engines like Unity (U) and Unreal has lowered the barrier for entry in creating new games. Presumably, artificial intelligence will further lower the cost of game development going forward, opening the door to additional competition.

EA is in a position to be disrupted, and at the valuation private equity is offering to pay here, any disruption could make this deal look like a strategic misstep.

It's hard to get a real sense of what sorts of cash flow EA's IPs can generate over the long term. You can guess what The Sims will generate next year or in 2027, sure. But if EA refuses to invest in making a Sims 5, the cash flows from the franchise are likely to collapse at some point in the not-all-that-distant future. The sports games are probably the safest bet for long-term revenue generation, but even there, some of the more aggressive monetization efforts like loot boxes could be curtailed by regulators going forward.

Long story short, EA generated $1.04 billion in net income over the past 12 months. EA would have to run as-is for close to 50 years for the PE buyer to get back their initial $50 billion outlay at that price. And given that EA's revenues have now stagnated and GAAP EPS has only modestly moved up over the past decade, I wouldn't count on growth making up for this lofty transaction multiple either. If anything, as Millennials get older and Gen Z moves into the prime gaming demographic, EA's profits may decline if the company can't improve its connection to younger gamers.

EA has already trimmed its overhead costs and reduced risk while pushing monetization techniques aggressively. It's unclear to me what levers private equity could pull to improve profitability if and when they take control of EA. This deal just doesn't make a lot of sense to me intuitively.

And as for EA stock owners today, I see very little reason to stay in the game. I'd be highly surprised if anyone bid higher than $50 billion.

If the $50 billion deal closes, that is only a few percent upside from here. And if the deal falls through, I would expect shares to trade back to the $160s if not lower.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Breaking Down IEA's Oil Supply Report: Key Insights For Energy Investors
data.description:
Have you noticed the price at the pump lately? Those numbers reflect a complex global oil market undergoing significant transformation. The International Energy Agency’s latest report reveals a fundamental shift in supply/demand dynamics that every energy investor should understand.

Global oil production has reached unprecedented levels, creating a widening gap between supply and demand. This imbalance continues to expand as production growth significantly outpaces modest consumption increases. The result is expanding inventories that place persistent downward pressure on prices, creating challenging conditions for producers but potential opportunities for consumers.

The geographic source of this production surge tells an equally important story. Non-OPEC+ countries, particularly those in the Americas, drive most expansion. The United States, Brazil, Canada and Guyana collectively contribute the majority of global production growth. This regional concentration represents a fundamental power shift in global energy markets, reducing OPEC’s traditional pricing influence and market control.

Meanwhile, demand growth remains tepid compared to surging supply. Interestingly, forecasters disagree significantly about future consumption trends. The IEA projects modest demand growth, while OPEC maintains a more optimistic outlook. This forecaster divergence creates additional market uncertainty, complicating investment decisions across the energy sector.

Perhaps most concerning for producers, global oil inventories continue their steady climb. The IEA warns that without intervention, stocks will build at unsustainable rates through year-end. These inventory builds typically precede periods of price weakness, creating potential headwinds for energy-focused investments dependent on stronger price environments.

For energy investors, these dynamics require careful consideration. The persistent supply/demand imbalance suggests continued price pressure through 2025 and beyond. Companies with lower production costs, reduced debt levels and operational flexibility may navigate these challenges more successfully than highly leveraged producers dependent on higher prices.

The current environment rewards selectivity rather than broad sector exposure. Understanding these fundamental shifts in market dynamics helps investors position appropriately for the evolving energy landscape where traditional assumptions about market balance and price support may no longer apply.
